sectionA.question: The movement of materials into an organization from suppliers is known as ______.
sectionA.explanation: Inbound logistics is the movement of materials from suppliers into the organisation. It is also called inward logistics.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 3, 3.2 - Role of Logistics

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not a component of trust?
sectionA.explanation: The components of trust are truth, equality, faithfulness, honesty, and capability. Obedience is not one of them.
sectionA.reference: Unit 4, Session 3, 3.2 - Components of trust

sectionA.question: The aim of moving materials efficiently with short journeys and appropriate equipment is known as ______.
sectionA.explanation: Material handling is about moving materials efficiently. It uses short journeys and the right equipment.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 3, 3.3.6 - Material handling

sectionA.question: The movement of materials from the organization to customers is termed as ______.
sectionA.explanation: Outward logistics is the movement of materials from the organisation to customers. It is also called outbound logistics.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 3, 3.2 - Role of Logistics

sectionA.question: Conflict that exists between the members of a department or a group or between the subgroups that consists of a large group resulting in structural relationship, goal, task or process is known as ______ conflict.
sectionA.explanation: Intragroup conflict happens within a department or group. It can involve subgroups or members of the same team.
sectionA.reference: Unit 4, Session 5, 5.2 - Nature of buyer-supplier conflicts

sectionA.question: One way in which a buying organization may protect itself against fluctuating commodity prices are through traded options. It is sometimes erroneously said that 'put' and 'call' options are opposite sides of the same transaction. An option conferring the right to buy is known as ______.
sectionA.explanation: A Call option gives the right to buy. It protects the buyer against rising prices.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 6, 6.3 - Traded options

sectionA.question: The integration and coordination of procurement requirements across the worldwide business unit -looking at common items, strategic and is part of the corporate plan is commonly referred to as ______.
sectionA.explanation: Global Sourcing integrates and coordinates procurement across worldwide business units. It is strategic and part of the corporate plan.
sectionA.reference: Unit 6, Session 1, 1.2 - Global sourcing/procurement

sectionA.question: A logistics network that consists of suppliers, manufacturing centres, warehousing, distribution centres, retail outlets, customers as well as raw materials, work-in-progress inventory and finished products that flow between ______. (ilities is termed ______).
sectionA.explanation: This is the definition of a Supply Chain. It is the network of facilities and the flow of materials between them.
sectionA.reference: Unit 1, Session 1, 1.2 - Definition of Supply Chain

sectionA.question: Which of the following is a driver of external risk?
sectionA.explanation: Interest rates are an external financial risk. They are outside the organisation's control.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 3, 3.4 - Drivers of internal and external risks

sectionA.question: The process of efficiently overseeing the constant flow of goods of materials into and out of an existing inventory is known as ______.
sectionA.explanation: This is the definition of Inventory Management. It is about overseeing the flow of goods in and out of inventory.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 2, 2.2 - Inventory Management and Its Objectives

sectionA.question: The purchasing pattern or demand distribution which is characterised by large transaction and uncertainty in demand overtime is termed as ______ demand.
sectionA.explanation: Sporadic or random demand has large transactions and uncertainty. It includes periods of zero demand followed by surges.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 2, 2.5.1 - Random or sporadic or intermittent

sectionA.question: The form of countertrade in which there is a transfer of unused or unusable credit balance in one country to overcome an imbalance of money by a trading partner in another country is termed ______.
sectionA.explanation: Switch trading transfers unused credit balances. A third party buys the credits to overcome imbalances.
sectionA.reference: Unit 6, Session 3, 3.1 - Countertrade

sectionA.question: The logistics activity that protects the materials from damage during the shifting or moving of materials is called ______.
sectionA.explanation: Packaging protects materials from damage during movement. It is an important logistics activity.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 3, 3.3.8 - Packaging

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a successful project management?
sectionA.explanation: Complexity and ambiguity are not characteristics of successful project management. They are challenges to overcome.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 1, 1.4 - Characteristics of successful project management

sectionA.question: The activity related to the movement of end-of-life or end-of-use products (waste) from their point of consumption to the point of production to capture value from the waste or proper disposal is termed as ______.
sectionA.explanation: Reverse logistics moves waste products back up the supply chain. This captures value or ensures proper disposal.
sectionA.reference: Unit 3, Session 3, 3.8 - Reverse Logistics

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not a factor of the Kenichi Ohmae 3C model?
sectionA.explanation: The Three Cs are Customer, Corporation, and Competition. Competence is not one of the factors.
sectionA.reference: Unit 1, Session 4, 4.3 - The Ohmae's Three Cs

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not an example of a risk identification technique?
sectionA.explanation: Network Analysis is a project planning technique. It is not a risk identification technique.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 3, 3.2 - Risk identification techniques - examples

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not a risk analysis technique?
sectionA.explanation: Scenario Analysis is a risk identification technique. Scenario Modelling is not listed as a risk analysis technique.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 3, 3.3 - Risk analysis methods and techniques - examples

sectionA.question: Buying is a term used to describe the purchase or acquisition of an item or service that's paid for via an exchange of money or another asset. supply chain management involves buying the goods and services that enable an organization to operate profitably and ethically. Which of these is not a buying technique commonly used in commodities trading?
sectionA.explanation: Indifference pricing is a concept, not a buying technique. The techniques are time budgeting, cost averaging/budget buying, and dynamic programming.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 6, 6.1 - Some Buying techniques in commodities trading

sectionA.question: Which of the following is not a driver of internal risk?
sectionA.explanation: Interest rates are an external risk driver. The others are internal risk drivers.
sectionA.reference: Unit 5, Session 3, 3.4 - Drivers of internal and external risks

answer.introduction: Cross-docking is a logistics procedure that eliminates storage and order picking.

mainPoints.heading: Definition of Cross-docking
mainPoints.detail: Cross-docking is a logistics procedure that eliminates storage and order picking.

mainPoints.heading: How It Works
mainPoints.detail: Products are received at a distribution centre and shipped out quickly without being stored.

answer.conclusion: Cross-docking reduces handling costs and speeds up delivery.
answer.reference: Unit 2, Session 2, 2.2 - Cross Docking in supply chain

subQuestions.label: B
subQuestions.question: An arrangement of different autonomous entities to achieve common objectives, where competition and teamwork exist in the market environment is identified as strategic alliance. Identify and explain the stages of strategic alliance in supply chain management.

answer.introduction: Strategic alliances are collaborative arrangements between autonomous entities. They involve four key stages in supply chain management.

mainPoints.heading: Alliance Conceptualisation
mainPoints.detail: Firms decide a collaborative arrangement is needed. They explore alternatives to current management.

mainPoints.heading: Alliance Pursuance
mainPoints.detail: The decision to form an alliance is made. Operational and strategic considerations are evaluated.

mainPoints.heading: Alliance Confirmation
mainPoints.detail: Partners are selected and confirmed. This requires 'the right decision at the right place.'

mainPoints.heading: Alliance Implementation/Continuity
mainPoints.detail: The alliance is put into action. Performance is assessed to decide if it continues or ends.

answer.conclusion: Each stage is critical for the success of a strategic alliance in supply chain management.
answer.reference: Unit 3, Session 5, 5.2 - The Stages of Strategic Alliance in Supply Chain Management

subQuestions.label: C
subQuestions.question: State and explain any four activities in the logistics system in the supply chains of organisations

answer.introduction: Logistics involves several key activities that ensure the smooth flow of materials through the supply chain.

mainPoints.heading: Procurement
mainPoints.detail: Finding suppliers, negotiating terms, and managing delivery.

mainPoints.heading: Inward Transport
mainPoints.detail: Moving materials from suppliers to the receiving area.

mainPoints.heading: Warehousing
mainPoints.detail: Storing materials and keeping them in good condition.

mainPoints.heading: Material Handling
mainPoints.detail: Moving materials efficiently with the right equipment.

answer.conclusion: These activities work together to ensure efficient logistics operations.

subQuestions.label: A
subQuestions.question: Supply chain is not a simple chain of business to manage, coupled with problems associated with one-to-one or business-to-business dealings. Supply chain managers are faced with a number of challenges. State and explain any four challenges supply chain managers face in the management of supply chains of organisations.

answer.introduction: Managing a supply chain is complex. Managers face many challenges that require careful attention.

mainPoints.heading: Difficulty Establishing Good Strategies
mainPoints.detail: New products create new supply chains. Strategies can clash and must be aligned with company goals.

mainPoints.heading: Cost of the System
mainPoints.detail: Designing and operating a supply chain with minimal costs is very difficult.

mainPoints.heading: System Maintenance
mainPoints.detail: Maintaining service levels while keeping costs low is a constant challenge.

mainPoints.heading: Uncertainty and Risk
mainPoints.detail: Demand, travel times, and equipment breakdowns are unpredictable. Outsourcing and lean manufacturing can increase risk.

answer.conclusion: Supply chain managers must navigate these challenges while maintaining efficiency and service levels.
answer.reference: Unit 1, Session 3, 3.3 - Challenges Faced by Supply Chain Managers

subQuestions.label: B
subQuestions.question: Kenichi Ohmae focuses on three key success factors for competitive advantage. Identify and explain these key factors in achieving competitive advantage.

answer.introduction: Ohmae's Three Cs model identifies three key factors that companies must focus on to achieve competitive advantage.

mainPoints.heading: Corporation
mainPoints.detail: The company must maximise its strengths compared to competitors. It should focus on its best functions.

mainPoints.heading: Customer
mainPoints.detail: The company must understand and satisfy customer needs. Customer interests should come before shareholder interests.

mainPoints.heading: Competition
mainPoints.detail: The company can compete through differentiation in areas like purchasing, design, and sales.

answer.conclusion: Balancing these three factors is essential for long-term competitive advantage.
answer.reference: Unit 1, Session 4, 4.3 - The Ohmae's Three Cs

subQuestions.label: C
subQuestions.question: Supply chain management relationship can be categorized into three main categories as vertical relationship management, horizontal relationship management and full collaboration relationship management. Explain each of the following relationship category in supply chain relationship management.

answer.introduction: Supply chain relationships can be categorized into three main types. Each type involves different levels of integration.

mainPoints.heading: Vertical Relationships
mainPoints.detail: Traditional links between firms in the chain. Examples are retailers, distributors, manufacturers, and suppliers.

mainPoints.heading: Horizontal Relationships
mainPoints.detail: Business arrangements between firms in parallel positions. They are at the same level of the chain.

mainPoints.heading: Full Collaboration
mainPoints.detail: Business arrangements that combine both vertical and parallel positions. This is the most integrated type.

answer.conclusion: Understanding these relationship types helps organizations choose the right level of integration.
answer.reference: Unit 1, Session 6, 6.2 - The Types of SCM Relationships

subQuestions.label: A
subQuestions.question: Explain the philosophical concept of just-in-time (JIT) in supply chain management.

answer.introduction: Just-in-time (JIT) is a philosophy that aims to eliminate waste and improve efficiency in supply chain management.

mainPoints.heading: Core Concept
mainPoints.detail: JIT means materials are ordered and delivered just before they are needed.

mainPoints.heading: Origins
mainPoints.detail: It was developed by Toyota to reduce waste and improve quality.

mainPoints.heading: Waste Elimination
mainPoints.detail: The system helps organise, plan, and control production effectively. It minimises waste in time, labour, and storage space.

mainPoints.heading: Benefits
mainPoints.detail: Companies become more responsive to customer orders. Benefits include better communication, more flexibility, and higher quality.

answer.conclusion: JIT is a powerful philosophy that focuses on continuous improvement and waste reduction.

subQuestions.label: B
subQuestions.question: State and explain any four differences between traditional and electronic procurement.

answer.introduction: Traditional and electronic procurement differ in several key aspects, particularly in cost focus and process automation.

mainPoints.heading: Cost Focus
mainPoints.detail: Traditional procurement reduces unit prices. E-procurement reduces transaction costs.

mainPoints.heading: Contract Management
mainPoints.detail: Traditional procurement minimises contract staff. E-procurement automates processes.

mainPoints.heading: Planning
mainPoints.detail: Traditional procurement plans supply. E-procurement plans and forecasts demand.

mainPoints.heading: Customer Focus
mainPoints.detail: Traditional procurement supports existing customers. E-procurement helps establish new customers.

answer.conclusion: E-procurement offers significant advantages over traditional methods in terms of cost and efficiency.
answer.reference: Unit 2, Session 6 - From Traditional procurement to E-Procurement

subQuestions.label: C
subQuestions.question:
Define the following terms as used in inventory management:
i. Components and Parts
ii. Maintenance, repairs, and operating inventories
iii. Capital goods

answer.introduction: Inventory management involves classifying different types of inventory. Each type serves a specific purpose.

mainPoints.heading: Components and Parts
mainPoints.detail: These are items that are assembled into final products. They are part of raw materials or sub-assemblies.

mainPoints.heading: Maintenance, Repairs, and Operating Inventories
mainPoints.detail: These include spare parts and supplies. They are used to maintain and repair equipment.

mainPoints.heading: Capital Goods
mainPoints.detail: These are equipment and assets that are not used up quickly. They have long useful lives and depreciate over time.

answer.conclusion: Proper classification of inventory is essential for effective inventory management.
answer.reference: Unit 3, Session 2, 2.3 - Classifications and Types of Inventory

subQuestions.label: A
subQuestions.question: Distinguish between responsiveness and efficiency as used in the context of supply chains.

answer.introduction: Responsiveness and efficiency are two key objectives in supply chain management that often involve trade-offs.

mainPoints.heading: Responsiveness
mainPoints.detail: Responsiveness means reacting quickly to customer needs. It focuses on speed and flexibility.

mainPoints.heading: Efficiency
mainPoints.detail: Efficiency means operating at the lowest possible cost. It focuses on minimising waste and costs.

mainPoints.heading: Trade-off
mainPoints.detail: These are often trade-offs. A highly efficient supply chain may not be very responsive.

answer.conclusion: Organizations must find the right balance between responsiveness and efficiency based on their market needs.
answer.reference: Unit 3, Session 2, 2.4 - Why Do Firms Keep Inventory?

subQuestions.label: B
subQuestions.question: State and explain four reasons why contractual relationship may be terminated in supply chain management.

answer.introduction: Contractual relationships in supply chains may end for various reasons. Understanding these reasons helps manage relationships better.

mainPoints.heading: Inadequate Understanding of Partnership
mainPoints.detail: Partners may not truly understand what partnership requires.

mainPoints.heading: Changes in Business Direction
mainPoints.detail: The partnership may no longer be valuable to either party.

mainPoints.heading: Product Obsolescence
mainPoints.detail: The product or service becomes outdated with no replacement.

mainPoints.heading: Economic Factors
mainPoints.detail: A supplier may become financially unstable and unable to meet objectives.

answer.conclusion: Regular assessment of partnerships helps identify potential issues before they lead to termination.

subQuestions.label: C
subQuestions.question: In today business setups, the supply chain plays an important role in increasing the value of the entire production process of businesses. State and explain any four components of supply chain management.

answer.introduction: Supply chain management involves several key components that work together to create value.

mainPoints.heading: Production
mainPoints.detail: Making products to meet consumer needs, demand, quality, and volume.

mainPoints.heading: Supply
mainPoints.detail: Obtaining required materials and inputs for production.

mainPoints.heading: Inventory
mainPoints.detail: Determining the appropriate level of inventory to increase competitiveness.

mainPoints.heading: Transportation
mainPoints.detail: Moving products through the supply chain to customers.

answer.conclusion: Each component is essential for an effective and efficient supply chain.
answer.reference: Unit 3, Session 1, 1.2 - Components of Supply Chain Management
